title: Signal Dry Bulk Weekly Report
summary:
Main
The freight market continues to gain momentum with a recovery seen in all main dry sizes and ballasters' view, with larger vessels driving the recent euphoria seen. In the iron ore market, the Chinese sticking to their strategy for control of iron ore prices as they have started now to plot a new plan for the creation of a single state-backed platform. The aim is all iron ore purchases to be completed through the platform in order to stabilize the increase of prices.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The freight market continues to gain momentum with a recovery seen in all main dry sizes and ballasters' view, with larger vessels driving the recent euphoria seen. In the iron ore market, the Chinese sticking to their strategy for control of iron ore prices as they have started now to plot a new plan for the creation of a single state-backed platform.

children.title: SECTION 1 - FREIGHT - Market Rates ($/t) - Firmer
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes
Image reference: ch1.png | assets/2022-02-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch1_11a0311e7e52.png
Capesize Brazil to NChina rates are on increase but still hover below $25/t. Panamax Continent to Far East rates that surpassed the barrier of $40/t with a spike at $46.5/t last week are now at soft lower levels of $45.6/t.

children.title: Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas
Image reference: ch2.png | assets/2022-02-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch2_4b7b16598e6d.png
The ballasters' view has now confirmed the downward correction that was signaled since last week in the Capesize and Panamax sectors. In the Capesize segment, the number of vessels sailing in ballast status has now started to drop below the one-year average of 80 vessels, while in the Panamax, the trend is moving near to the average of 100 vessels.

children.title: Increasing
children.summary:
Image reference: ch3.png | assets/2022-02-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch3_baee3b3c9369.png
The dry bulk demand evolution eventually started to increase for the large vessel sizes, while the ongoing threat on the wheat supply market stemming from Russian-Ukraine tensions is paving the way for higher Supramax tondays growth. In the Handysize segment, it seems that the demand is growing at a slower mode following the high recorded at Week 4, however, it is still firmer than the extreme lows at the start of the year.

children.title: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
section_path: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.section_path_text: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.summary:
Image reference: ch4.png | assets/2022-02-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch4_7d8d82877cf5.png
The last days of February reversed the sudden increase that was recorded in the previous week and we see now a decrease with levels , almost to below the threshold of 1,000 vessels. The overall number of ship congestion is now around 930 vessels, with the handysize segment holding its high levels of congestion~150 vessels, while in the supramax, we see a trend of decreasing below 250 vessels.
